title: Method Dispatch and the Class Precedence List

sections.markdown:
# Method Dispatch and the Class Precedence List

When a generic function is called, CLOS:

1. Looks at the actual classes of the arguments.
2. Computes the **applicable methods** — those whose specialisers each accept the corresponding argument's class.
3. Sorts them from **most specific to least specific** using each class's **class precedence list (CPL)**.
4. By default, calls the most specific one.

The CPL is a deterministic linearisation of a class and all its ancestors. For single inheritance it's just `(class parents... standard-object t)`. For multiple inheritance, CLOS uses the C3 algorithm (similar to Python). The CPL is stable: every class has exactly one.

You can inspect it:

```lisp
(class-precedence-list (find-class 'integer))
;; => (#<BUILT-IN-CLASS INTEGER> #<...RATIONAL> #<...REAL> #<...NUMBER> #<...T>)
```

Multiple inheritance teaser:

```lisp
(defclass a () ())
(defclass b () ())
(defclass c (a b) ())   ; CPL: C, A, B, STANDARD-OBJECT, T
```

A method specialised on `A` and one on `B` are both applicable to a `C` instance; the `A` method wins because `A` precedes `B` in the CPL of `C` (left-to-right order in the superclass list).

This is why **method order matters** and why you should think about your inheritance graph before reaching for diamond patterns.

sections.code: (progn (defclass a () ()) (defclass b (a) ()) (defclass c (b) ()) (defgeneric f (x)) (defmethod f ((x a)) :a) (defmethod f ((x b)) :b) (f (make-instance 'c)))
sections.explain: C inherits from B which inherits from A. Both methods are applicable; B is more specific.

sections.code: (progn (defclass left () ()) (defclass right () ()) (defclass child (left right) ()) (defgeneric f (x)) (defmethod f ((x left)) :left) (defmethod f ((x right)) :right) (f (make-instance 'child)))
sections.explain: CHILD's CPL puts LEFT before RIGHT (left-to-right in (defclass child (left right) ())), so :LEFT wins.

FIND-CLASS looks up the class metaobject; CLASS-NAME goes back the other way. (To inspect the full CPL you'd use SB-MOP:CLASS-PRECEDENCE-LIST, or CLOSER-MOP for portability — outside CL's standard package.)

sections.prompt: Define classes BASE, MID (extends BASE), and LEAF (extends MID). Define generic function WHO with methods on each that return :BASE, :MID, :LEAF respectively. Define DEMO that returns the result for a fresh LEAF.
